author: L.K.
title: 内卷严重但是很值得学的一门CS课（A very useful and very competitive CS course）
comment_content:
--- Before Midterm ---

Part 1 - Logic：
  逻辑运算符、逻辑等价、逻辑推理等
Part 2 - Sets and functions：
  集合与函数与不可数集
Part 3 - Number theory：
  带余除法、同余数关系、密码学基础等
Part 4 - Algorithms：
  算法基础（Big Theta, Big Oh, Big Omega）
  最坏分析，以及常见算法。

--- After Midterm ---

Part 5 - Induction and recursion：
  a. 归纳法（强归纳与弱归纳）
  b. 递归证明（结构归纳法）
  c. 递归定义（集合、字符串、二叉树）
Part 6 - Counting：
  排列组合、鸽笼原理、二项式、排容原理等
Part 7 - Probability：
  概率、条件概率、贝叶斯定理、随机变量等
Part 8 - Graph：
  有向／无向图、欧拉回路、欧拉路径等

author: HakkaEason
title: a course which clearly shows that understanding concepts and solving problems are different.
comment_content:
Those concepts themselves apart from a few are quite simple and interesting (and some seems familiar as some of the DSE ICT and Maths topics), you can understand all of them in 2-3 days by doing revision (premise: if you have listened to all the lectures and seen those lecture notes). However, understanding those concepts is just the beginning. COMP2711 is really about manipulating problems until you can solve them using the concepts you were taught in class.

Also, understanding concepts ≠ able to solve everything. Try solving previous problems to get familiar with the "ideas" is the main point.
So basically the process would be like this:
understand concepts -> practice solving problems -> deal with those questions in exam papers
Topics:
Before midterm:
1. Propositional Logic, Predicate Logic, Inference and Proof
2. Sets and Functions
3. Counting, Permutations and Combinations, Binomial Coefficients
4. Inclusion and Exclusion
5. Conditional Probability and Bayes’ Theorem
After midterm:
6. Expectation and Variance
7. Number Theory and Modular Arithmetic
8. GCDs, Congruences and The Chinese Remainder Theorem
9. Cryptography
10. Algorithms
11. Induction
12. Recursion
comment_teaching:
Professor Leung (nickname: Kenneth) is already doing well at teaching, explanation with details on examples and proofs, willing to answer students' questions, also he did extra stuff so that we could have more questions to practice.
And there is very frequent interaction in our telegram group as there are TA and (very smart and knowledgeable) students always discussing some difficult problems, even some advanced problems. And they always answer our questions which really help us to understand how to solve those problems.
comment_grading:
I have heard that grading is a bit bad, like C range for mean in the past, but hopefully they would be more lenient in this sem.

Zoom Polls (10%) : 16/16
counts best 2/3 of polls (e.g. 24 polls in total then count best 16 polls).
1 mark for each poll, 0.5 mark for the wrong answer, 0 mark for not answering.
one poll for each lecture, so you can treat it as "attendance".
polling questions are actually very easy.

Midterm (25%): 21/50 (below about 1sd)

Written Homework (25%): 468/500
counts best 5/6 of HWs (6 HWs in total).
homework mainly test your basic knowledge of those concepts and test your solving ability.
In fact, most of the solutions to those questions in hw can be found online, you can double-check your answer with it.

Final Exam (40%): 42/80, above mean(40.9) a little bit

Total: 65.X% (mean about 68.X%)
Grade: B-
7X% should be B
A cut off at about 84~85%
the grading seems more lenient to low grade, might be increased 0.5~1 subgrade by comparing to previous grading, but high grade e.g. B+ or above seems still need to get 78% or above to secure.
comment_workload:
Each Homework may need 2-3 days to finish if you want to get a high score securely.
Doing revision isn't very hard and time-consuming. But make sure you have understood all the concepts, proofs, theorems (they are very important to remember as mostly they would be the key for you to do proving and solving) and examples(such as Monty Hall Problem and Towers of Hanoi, sometimes they would adjust those examples and put them into exams)
For the exams, I highly recommend doing every exercise and past paper they have provided. As you don't know how to do those questions because:
1. Some of the questions are mixed topics, you might not know how many/which concepts you need to use.
2. Sometimes you have to "guess" those questions, such as while doing a combinatorial proof or doing an induction step, you have to know what is the question saying and what the question requires you to do.
3. New question type is the biggest problem as you might have never seen it and have zero experience with it.
Therefore, you have to do a lot of exercises to improve your solving and understanding abilities, also for you to experience different kinds of questions so that you would have similar ideas with similar questions in the exam.

It would be a hard time for you a few days before the exam, as you would do a lot of papers, exercises and questions. Most of the time you would have brainstorm while solving those problems you don't understand and are not familiar with.

And btw, I recommend taking COMP2711 in fall better than in spring. In spring, there will be keener competition and harder difficulty in exam papers

author: solarski22
title: Hard exams and bad grades in spring.
comment_content:
Satisfying and interesting content. In terms of content, this course is good. It contains some math and CS stuffs, will be quite hard for people like me who are not good at maths.

Logic
Inference and proofs
Sets and functions
Number theory
Cryptography
Algorithmic analysis
Induction and Recursion
Counting, permutations and combinations
Binomial coefficients
Inclusion-exclusion Principle
Probability and Random variables
Graph Theory
comment_teaching:
Prof. Yi teaches quite well. Not boring, sometimes even interesting. However, some of the points he gone through was not deep enough, he might just use a simple example to explain sth that will be much more complex when it is in the exam. His ppt slides are sometimes too simple. He explained some complex stuffs quite well, and he is kind and nice to answer your questions. One more point, he sometimes write notes on his slides on his iPad, but his handwriting is a bit messy, his writings will overlap with the words on the slides and he will erase part of it to further explain sth else, which is hard to read/understand, sometimes you have to rewatch the video to understand what he is talking about. Overall, his teaching is good.

The TAs are wonderful. They are helpful, nice, and are able to teach well. All TAs this year are very good. Sometimes if you got questions about your hw, you can feel free to email them and discuss with them, they can offer you good explanations. They did really well this year. FYI there are 5 TAs and they will each be responsible for 2 to 3 tutorials.
comment_grading:
For spring semester, if you are Y1 student or you have not got a major yet, then I strongly advice you to take this course later, otherwise, your CGA will have a lot of uncertainties, esp if you are are not very good at maths...

If you are a math genius, then just take any course you want, you don't have to care the grading part & this review.

THE EXAMS ARE REALLY REALLY HARD. You cannot imagine how the knowledge learnt in class can be applied to some of the questions. The logic flow of those questions are hard to follow, especially for year 1 students, the exams are definitely not the ones you encountered in MATH 1013/1014... Btw the pre-requisite is not accurate, MATH 1013/4 don't help much here.

Homework 20%
Mid-Term Exam 30%
Final Exam 50%

The Homework part is not very difficult, and you should try your best to get as high marks as possible in you homework. However, even if you could score full marks in your hw, it means nth to the exams, as the exam questions are a lot harder than the hw questions. This situation is not that significant in the midterm but very severe in the final. Every question is like bonus level. I heard someone else saying that the final questions are harder than 2711H in fall (absolutely harder than 2711 in fall too), you can take a look at them in the attached files below. I personally agree with it.

my stat:
Midterm: Touch mean
Final: 10 marks lower than mean (over 100)
HW: only one HW is below mean, others are nearly full
Grade = C range
comment_workload:
Homework + Revision/understanding + Do past paper
The workload is not too big, but not small either.
Adequate for a 4 credit course.

comment_teaching: Professor Yi Ke is from mainland China but his English is fluent. He is patient to take questions and reply to all the e-mails so he is a good instructor overall. Even though I am not good at this course I am still grateful for his teaching. Although I kind of F off both exams and found it a relief not having 2711 anymore, I would still say that going to the office and discuss with Prof Yi has helped me get a better insight into those materials. He is always there to help answer our questions and it's really kind of him.
comment_grading:
For us, the grading scheme is 20% homework, 30% midterm, 50% final.
Just finished the midterm exam so cannot say much. The questions are not that hard if you are familiar with all the contents and being well prepared for reference (hard-copy) material (yes there is no online material allowed and I didn't realize it until the exam day). The time is not sufficient for me, due to the poor preparation of materials and poor strategy. But it is always the case that you should seize every possible point. This course is not friendly if you are careless (i am careless :(().
-----------------------------------------------------------------
Oh, I am back commenting on the final. The final exam has two parts, again. They focus on the second half of the material, from induction to graph. They tend to have questions that require knowledge of several areas (e.g. graph combined with probability and expectation etc), so we have to make quick reactions to them. The time is tight for me again, and for us there are a lot of scores regarding the graph theory, which surprised me a lot. The mean for the 2 parts of the final is 31/52, 23/46, so the exam is not pleasant at all. However, for hard questions, you should not just quit them. Just writing some random stuff that shows your understanding can give you unexpected partial points!! I didn't realize this since I was pretty nervous.
comment_workload: The workload is not big theoretically, for there are only 6 assignments (you are expected to submit the answer as a single pdf file). However, I have difficulties digesting them so I did turn to its textbook for extra content and had spent a lot of time reading the course note. Before class, I strongly suggest that you preview the content or you might have difficulties understanding some challenging part of it, or you might not realize what are the real important contents, making your lecture time inefficient. Also, be serious to the tutorial questions as they are inspiring as well.

author: NaturalEthan
title: Instructor matters
comment_content:
Since it's a CCC course, the course's coverage is wide. It includes:
Basic logic
Basic modular arithmetic
Cryptography, RSA, etc.
Recursion and Induction
Basic probability: Expectation, Variance, etc.
Combination: binomial theory, etc.

Those knowledge is well applicated in the CS area and will be covered in advanced courses like COMP 3711. It's a CS required course. So try your best to learn this course.
comment_teaching:
Professor TAI, Chiew Lan may be not suitable for this course. She teaches with an unclear mind, shows little logic in her teaching.  I found it's much easier to self-study via the lecture notes than listening to her teaching. She wrote in a messy way, not to mention sometimes she seems don't understand what's the meaning of the lecture notes.
However, I'd like to give a thumb to Professor YI, Ke. I took some of his lectures around the corner of the final exam, and I found he has a clear logic and he can explain the knowledge in a clear, fluent and easy way. His writing well corresponds to what he is speaking. In the end, you will find that Professor YI taught more contents in a 80 minutes class than Professor Tai, and you will find it more interesting and less confusing in Professor YI's class.
comment_grading: Fairly speaking, the grade is strict, but not fierce. You can view the grading scheme in other users reviews, and what I want to suggest is that you should digest the tutorial and lecture notes well, and do the past paper as much as possible.
comment_workload: Medium I think. There are 6 homework. They are inspiring. Take the homework seriously.

author: rkcw
comment_content:
Syllabus:

Pre-midterm:
Logic:
- Propositional logic
- Predicate logic
- Inference and proofs
Sets and functions:
- Injection, Surjection, Bijection
- Cardinality
Induction and recursion:
- Induction, strong induction, double induction, structural induction
- Recursive definition and step
Number Theory:
- Modular arithmetic
- GCDs and congruences (Chinese remainder theorem)
- Cryptography (Extended Euclidean algorithm, RSA)

Post-midterm:
Algorithms:
- Big Θ, big O, big Ω notations
Counting:
- Basic counting (Sum rule, product rule)
- Permutations and combinations
- Binomial coefficients (Pascal's identity)
- Inclusion-exclusion principle
Probability:
- Intro to probability (Expected value, variance)
- Conditional probability (Baye's theorem)
- Random variables (Binomial distribution, geometric distribution)
Graphs:
- Graphs (Bipartite, K, C)
P.S. order of topics may change from semester to semester, but the overall content doesn't change much.

This course teaches the fundamentals of logical reasoning and such, good and useful content.
Favours people who took M1/M2 in DSE (or just people with background in statistics/probability in general)
comment_teaching:
First half is taught by Yi Ke. Yi Ke's teaching makes already convoluted topics even harder to understand. You really gotta study hard for midterms because you can't rely on his teachings alone.

Second half is taught by Dr. James Tsz Him Cheung. James teaches way better than Yi Ke and the things he talks about actually makes sense. Also eager to help students after lectures end.

Arman is one of the TAs which is always fun, but tutorials don't count attendance so can be skipped.
comment_grading:
Grading:
In-class quizzes: 10%
HW: 20% (5 HWs)
Midterm: 30% (1 A4 sized cheat sheet)
Finals: 40% (2 A4 sized cheat sheets)
Total: 100%
In-class quizzes are done on Canvas during class. You only need to get an average of 80% correct in quizzes to get the full 10% in-class quizzes marks.
Technically, if you have friends in the same class, you can ask them to tell you when the quizzes are posted and do them remotely and skip lectures.

Statistics:
All in-class quizzes and all 5 HWs:
- UQ, Median, LQ: 90%+
- Mean: lower because outliers (zeroes)
Midterms:
- UQ: 78.5, Median: 64, Mean: 60.62, LQ: 47.5
- High: 100, Low: 0, SD: 19.78
Finals:
- UQ: 71.25, Median: 59.5, Mean: 58.01, LQ: 44
- High: 96, Low: 12, SD: 17.92

Grading is fair imo, but you have to get 100%, or at least close to, for HWs and in-class quizzes due to the high averages (you can always chatgpt them).

Grades:
Quizzes: full 10%
HW: 96.6/100
Midterms and finals: 1.5 SDs above mean
Grade: barely 90% (A+)

Friend got ~90% in HW, mean in midterms and LQ in finals and still got B-, so B range is very big.
comment_workload:
5 HWs with varying lengths, some HWs (*cough cough* induction) will take you forever to do, while others can be done within 2-3 hours at most.

Since the counting and probability taught are very fundamental, people with any background in statistics will find those topics extremely simple.
For people without background, a lot of extra work might be required to catch up to people who do.

For midterms and finals, not much can be done for revision other than doing past papers due to most questions being proof-based (other than probability and counting). Cheat sheets are only there to help you remember the sheer number of laws and theorems learnt throughout the course.

author: HTGAzureX1212
title: A Proper Introduction to Computer Science
comment_content:
Topics covered:
1. Propositional logic
2. Predicate logic
3. Inference and proofs
4. Sets and functions
5. Counting
6. Combinations
7. Binomial coefficients
8. Inclusion-exclusion principle
9. Introduction to probability
10. Conditional probability & Bayes' theorem
11. Random variables
12. Modular arithmetic
13. Greatest common divisor
14. Cryptography
15. Algorithm analysis
16. Mathematical induction
17. Recursion
18. Graphs

These topics are *actual* introductions to computer science, unlike a certain other course called COMP1021 (follow-up review from COMP1021 review).
comment_teaching:
Both professors are great instructors.

Prof. Leung teaches at a faster pace and uses simple and interesting examples to communicate ideas and concepts in the course clearly.

The main TA (Arman) is an interesting guy.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme:

In-class exercises: 10%
Assignments: 25%
Midterm examination: 25%
Final examination: 25%

My score:
In-class exercises: 9.42/10
Midterm: 78/100
Final: 66/100
Overall: ~78/100

Letter grade: B
I'd say the grading is fine (I didn't spend much time on it), I think this course is graded on a curve.
About 80% for a B+, I am unaware of the A range cutoff.

Midterm exam was of reasonable difficulty and the final exam is harder with fewer hints in the questions.
comment_workload:
The workload is fair for a 4-credit course.

In-class exercises are great so you don't just leave the classroom after the lecture without doing anything to consolidate.

Only 4 pieces of assignments this semester but they are *interesting* and take some time to complete, but doesn't take *that* long to complete anyway.

One of the UGTAs also makes "boredom" questions for us to think about and apply knowledge learned in less apparent ways.

author: Kabul
title: Elementary content, mind-blowing exams
comment_content:
There is a combination of topics in different areas of discrete maths. Most of them, except the cryptography and the algorithm analysis part, are not very computer science-oriented. You can treat this course as an extension of your high-school maths.

Topics included:
0️⃣ Introduction to Discrete Mathematics

1️⃣ Logic
      🔶 1 Propositional Logic
      🔶 2 Predicate Logic
      🔶 3 Inference and Proofs
      🔶 4 Sets and Functions

2️⃣ Number Theory
      🔶 5 Modular Arithmetic
      🔶 6 GCDs and Congruences
      🔶 7 Cryptography

3️⃣ Combinatorics
      🔶 8 Counting
      🔶 9 Permutations and Combinations
      🔶 10 Binomial Coefficients
      🔶 11 Inclusion-Exclusion Principle
      🔶 12 Analysis of Algorithms

4️⃣ Probability Theory
      🔶 13 Introduction to Probability
      🔶 14 Conditional Probability and Bayes' Theorem
      🔶 15 Expectation and Variance

5️⃣ Induction and Recurrence
      🔶 16 Induction
      🔶 17 Recursion

6️⃣ Graphs
      🔶 18 Graphs
comment_teaching:
Prof PAPADOPOULOS had a heavy Greek accent but it was still largely intelligible. He mostly lingered around the basic concepts and intuition, suitable for math noobs like me. Most importantly, he's fucking charming, with his athletic body build and visually appealing beard. Guys plz go ahead and 食洋腸. Hehe~😋

In comparison, Prof WANG's class (which shared the same set of teaching materials) drilled down to the more pure-math aspect of the topics. I recommend Prof WANG's lectures if you're mathematically mature and would like to have exposure to more advanced content.

The only thing I'd complain about was the difficulty gap between the content taught in class and the exams. I believe most of us were not equipped to do well in the exams even we were given these questions banks and past papers. The exams this year were soooo different from the past.
comment_grading:
Assessment scheme:
✏️ 20% Quizzes
✏️ 30% Mid-Term Exam
✏️ 50% Final Exam

The final score on the quizzes will be calculated from the sum of the 4 highest scores out of these 5 quizzes. A number of in-class exercises will also be given and they will at most give you a boost of 1 sub-grade.

✏️ Midterm
Mean = 35.7/60
Max = 59.5/60

✏️ Final
Mean = 48.2/81
SD = 17.2
Max = 78/81

While the professor taught easy content in class, both the midterm and the final were on a completely new level of difficulty. The exams this year required the synthesis and integration of multiple topics and there were quite a few "intriguing" questions that were totally out of my expectation.

One question in the midterm asked us to combine the 2 logic statements given by the knight and the knave. Most of us habitually formulated the logic statement by using inference ("→"), which turned out to be wrong if we carefully thought about the truth table values. Another question asked us to give a proof that every natural number can be written as a unique product of an odd component and a non-negative integer power of 2 (in fact the question was written in a more sophisticated manner, combined with notations of sets, divisibility, etc). From what I've heard the median score for that question across the 2 lecture sections was 0. I believe the vast majority of us had no idea of what the heck that question was going on LMAO.

The final was not easy either. "Basic" stuff included binomial distribution, structural induction (combined with GCDs), and the recursive definitions of the nCr function and the set of complete graphs. We also have a bunch of "interesting" questions such as the sequence formed by the number of ways the 3x1 tiles could be placed in a 3xn board and the proof of its recurrence relations, and another question on what values of m, n for a bipartite graph to be a Euler path but not a Euler circuit. No more mechanical mathematical induction. No more mechanical proof of recurrence relations. All these were implicitly packed into another interesting and deadly questions. Have fun and get wrecked in the final exam.

I got slightly above mean in the midterm, +1.14 SD above mean in the final and averaged 90-ish in the quizzes. Overall I got around 77.7%.

💯 Final grade: A-

The grading is very lenient. I could still marginally secure an A-range grade even though I'd crashed the midterm.
comment_workload: The workload of this course mainly lies on the quizzes and regular practice on past papers and tutorial discussion questions. But this year while practices are necessary, they are INSUFFICIENT for good grades. The more important part lies in the thorough understanding and the ability to synthesize and re-integrate different topics.

author: Argen
title: Depends on the Instructor
comment_content:
Just like the title, Discrete Math for Computer Science.

If you are Engineering/Science/Math student, you will find some of the content similar to other courses (e.g. counting and probability are very basic), while some of them are designed for CS students only, like modular arithmetic (mod, gcd, RSA), algorithm analysis using big O/theta/omega. Not very difficult if you have a good Math background and are familiar with their proof.

The detailed topics:
Logic (Proposition/quantifier)
Proof (Direct/contraposition/contradiction)
Set and function
Modular arithmetic
Counting
Probability
Algorithm analysis
Induction
Recursion
Graph
comment_teaching: TAI, Chiew Lan is my lecturer this semester. I would say she teaches quite badly. She expects students already having the knowledge (though some are true) and she just reads out from the powerpoint. Basically we learnt nothing in the lecture. She lacks preparation to the lecture notes, which leads to her "lagging" for a couple of minutes for the examples in class. She is an interactive lecturer, who asks challenging questions BEFORE teaching the main parts, and usually causing dead air for another couple of minutes throughout the class.
comment_grading:
This semester is a disaster.

Midterm is unexpectedly simple. Many students just get a full mark and the instructor claims "they deliberately do that and 80% of the final questions will have the same difficulty". Happily, the final exam reverses the difficulty, only 20% questions are like easy, while 80% questions are complicated or proofing with harsh marking scheme.

The TAs (especially mainlanders) tends to KILL you rather than giving you a good grade. For multiple times in the quizzes and final I have written for more than 15 lines (reasonable and logical, but some mistakes at the middle) for proving and eventually get a ZERO and comments like "WHAT?", "WHY?", "???" neglecting all the remaining part where I'm 100% sure correct.
comment_workload:
(For 2017 Fall only, because the course keeps changing and this may not be a good reference)

Pretty light.
1 midterm 1 final.
No assignment.
Weekly quiz with question pool provided. (For another lecture, biweekly quiz)
Inclass exercise

author: sgargaa
title: Good course
comment_content: There are 5 chapters that are tested in the final examination for this course. The first chapter is about the logic gates, sets, relations and functions. The second chapter is permutations and combinations. The third is about probability, which includes many theorems like Bayes' theorem etc. The fourth chapter tests cryptography and many other concepts about the same topic. The fifth and last chapter deals with concepts like mathematical induction. The highest weightage in the examinations is given to chapters 3 and 5.
comment_teaching: DYY is an excellent teacher and he uploads videos of his courses within 2 days. He teaches all concepts well and spends a good amount of time in class clearing the doubts of students.
comment_grading:
Grading is a bitch. The exams are pretty tough, yet the mean goes high.
10% assignments
30% midterm
60% final
comment_workload: There isn't much workload. 5 assignments in total. To be submitted once in every 2 weeks.

author: {Curly Braces}
title: Course with very Interesting Contents
comment_content:
The contents include:
1. Logic
  - Propositional Logic
  - Predicate Logic
  - Inference
2. Sets and Functions
3. Counting and Probability
  - Sum and Product Rule
  - Permutations, Combinations and Derangement
  - Binomial Coefficients and Binomial Theorem
  - Inclusion-Exclusion Principle
  - Probability and Conditional Probability
  - Bayes' Theorem
  - Expectation and Variance
4. Number Theory and Cryptography
  - Modular Arithmetic
  - Congruences and GCD
  - Chinese Remainder Theorem
  - Cryptography (Caesar/Affine Cipher, RSA Encryption)
5. Algorithm Analysis
  - Big-Theta, Big-Oh and Big-Omega Notations
  - Induction and Recurrence

Note that the graph theory is not included in this semester since there is not enough time.

To be honest, the contents of this course is quite interesting.
comment_teaching:
This semester is actually the first semester for Prof. Papadias teaching this course. Despite having a heavy accent, I personally like his teaching style, which makes me feel like watching a Youtube tutorial on Discrete Maths (but with interactions, of course).

Other than that, he teaches well and often ends the lesson early. He also makes quick responses to students for any questions, too.

(I skipped most of the tutorials so I cannot comment about the TAs lmao)
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme:
Homework: 30%
Quizzes: 15%
Polls: 10% (Counts the best 2/3 of the polls only)
Final Exam: 45%

This course is pretty much hard to get A-range for normal students since there would be some more big-brain students. Other than that, the grading is quite fair.

For reference:
Homework: 100% (All 5 homework got 100/100)
Quiz 1: 10/10
Quiz 2: 8/10
Polls: 100% (Got 15 of them correct, and there are total 22 polls)
Final Part 1: 17.25/30
Final Part 2: 20.5/30

Final Grade: B+ (81.8%)

As expected, the part the determine your grade the most is the final exam. I am just about 2-3 marks higher than mean for both parts of the final exam, which results in getting a B+.
comment_workload:
As mentioned in the Grades part, there is 5 homework to do in this semester, each of them consists of 5~6 questions. Each of them has some question that is challenging and may need some more time to think.
(In fact, some of the homework questions are actually taken from past papers lol)

For the polls, just make sure to attend every lesson, as you can still get half marks for each poll you get them wrong.

The difficulty of the quizzes is fair enough. The best thing is that they do not require us to join Zoom meeting for the quiz. But this does not mean the quiz would be too hard!

The final exam was actually out of my expectation. I expect the paper would be very long, but it ends up with only 3 questions in each part. But this does not mean the final exam is very easy. If you are not careful enough you can still fail. Because of this, doing past papers are important to tackle these careless mistakes.

author: !gpa_god
title: CS101 - Discrete Matematics
comment_content:
1. Logic (propositions, predicates, inferences and proving techniques)
2. Set theorem and functions
3. Number theory (mod arithmetic, GCD and congruence, cryptography)
4. Intro Algorithm analysis (big-O/theta/omega)
5. Combinatorics (counting, permutations, combinations, binomial coefficients, inclusion-exclusion)
6. Probability (conditional probability, random variables)

comment_teaching: Prof. Yi's English is not too shabby and is quite easy to follow. His occasional chokes are pretty hilarious too. Jokes aside, he is able to explain complex ideas pretty well. TAs are pretty helpful too.
comment_grading:
Homework 20% (best 5 out of 7 homework)
Mid-Term Exam 30%
Final Exam 50%

There are 7 homework in total, with around 4-5 questions, ranging from easy to moderately difficult. The mean for most of the homework are quite high (around 85% per homework).

Topics for the midterm differs from semester to semester. For this semester it was logic, set theory, function and number theory. The midterm was manageable but I fucked up by not thoroughly revising the topics before the midterm. The mean of the midterm was 75.6%, highest was 99.5 (fucking hell) and the lowest was 22.

As for the final exam, since all the lectures for this semester was delivered through online, the final exam was split into two parts and between each part there were 15 mins for people to upload their answers onto Canvas. The topics were mainly focused on point 4 to 7 from the aforementioned content list. Needless to say, this year's final exam was HARD AS FUCK and the mean reflects it.

Final exam (pt 1):
Mean: 27.6/53
High: 51
Low: 0

Final exam (pt 2):
Mean: 26.7/47
High: 46.5
Low: 0
comment_workload:
I don't think there's much workload for this course. There were only 7 homework, which only the best 5 would be counted towards the final grade.

However, you are expected to spend some time to revise after lectures cuz the midterm and the final exam were WAYYYYYY more difficult than the homework. Don't get too cocky if you got 90% or higher for the homework cuz you will be screwed in the midterm or final exam.

author: Yu Ming
title: Decent Course
comment_content: The content that is taught in COMP2711 is fundamental to computer science and provides a comprehensive spectrum of knowledge. It solidifies our foundation of mathematics and computer science and really trains our brain to function in a mathematical way.
comment_teaching: This lecturer is quite helpful as he actually teaches the content and explains the hard sections of the content. He would use whiteboard to draw diagrams to help us visualize the complex knowledge to ease it. Whenever we have questions, he would also answer the questions and address the needs of the students.
comment_grading: The professors are lenient on grading as some may have missed the first few exercises due to add/drop period, and they would allow late submissions and still count the scores. Other than that, the homeworks are also promptly graded, which is decent.
comment_workload: This course has quite a heavy workload as the materials are taught in a fast manner such that there is loads of self-study needed. Aside from the self study, there are mandatory in-class exercises to do in lectures. There is also homework every once a few weeks, but it is quite busy!

author: :)Reminder:(
comment_content: The course content covers core knowledge points including logic and proof, sets, functions, algorithms, counting, induction, recursion, probability theory, and graph theory. In addition, the course introduces propositional logic and the use of truth tables, which strengthen students’ ability to reason and construct rigorous arguments. This course has weak relevance to calculus, and the logical thinking it cultivates is of great help to the development of problem-solving skills in subsequent programming learning.
comment_teaching: The instructor explains the concepts in detail, emphasizing both theoretical understanding and practical applications. The teaching pace is moderate, and in-class examples are provided to reinforce comprehension. However, the in-class exercises are sometimes repetitive, focusing more on basic skills rather than challenging applications. Moreover, the results of in-class exercises are counted toward the final grade.
comment_grading: The grading scheme is relatively clear, consisting of homework, quizzes, a midterm, and a final exam. The exam difficulty is moderate, primarily testing fundamental understanding and basic problem-solving skills rather than highly complex questions. Overall, students who keep up with the course should be able to achieve good grades.
comment_workload: Each class includes in-class exercises that must be submitted on the same day. These exercises help students consolidate what they have learned, but may pose some pressure for those with weaker mathematical backgrounds. The professor has mentioned that they will try to help such students “survive” the exams. [However, if you are not good at mathematics or have weak logical thinking skills, this course is definitely not as easy as you might imagine.]

comment_teaching:
Prof YI is good at explaining concepts with proper examples.
Kind and patient. Even can answer questions in mandarin after class.

Dr. CHEUNG Tsz Him is also nice. Willing to answer questions after class or via email.
comment_grading:
In-class Quizzes: 10%
Homework 20%
Midterm Exam 30%
Final Exam 40%

The A range is probably fair enough. But the content is difficult. Not recommended to fulfill common core requirement.

In-class quizzes will be conducted by Canvas Quizzes.  The 20% lowest scores (including no submissions)  will be dropped in calculating your final quiz score.  Quizzes conducted during the first two weeks (add/drop period) will not be counted.

There will be 5 homework assignments throughout the course.  Submit a SOFTCOPY of your assignment to Canvas by the deadline. If your submission is a scan of a handwritten solution, make sure that it is of high enough resolution to be easily read. At least 300dpi and possibly denser.

Homework late Policy: 1 point for every hour late.

For the midterm and final exam, you will be allowed to bring one (midterm) or two (final) A4-sized cheat sheets. It can be either single-sided or double-sided, handwritten or printed.
comment_workload:
Rather heavy workload. Personally I will say the content is more difficult than Math1014 L1. And there are many real-life questions that you should interpret them first. Understanding is much more important because cheat sheet is allowed.
Another great but sad thing is that AI is clever enough to solve 99% questions.

author: saygex
title: COMP 2711 Summer
comment_content:
The course covers the same topics as in a regular semester (they didn't even skip graph theory like they did in the spring semester). But the homeworks and exams are extremely easy compared to that of normal semester. So it might be less preferable to someone who prefers rigor. But I assume those guys will take 2711H anyways.
Although there are many topics, they are very introductory and don't go much in depth (though the questions can be quite challenging), so you would likely need to take other courses such as MATH 4141, MATH 3121, and MATH 2421 to gain a deeper understanding. I believe there is significant overlap with MATH 2421, so if you are a DSCT, COSC, or just a student interested in these subjects, you should definitely consider taking them together.

One interesting finding: The syllabus is an exact copy of UC Berkeley's Math 55 https://math.berkeley.edu/courses/overview/lowerdivcourses/math55
This is actually a good thing since UCB is known for being a strong CS school, and I don't mind because I don't have to pay $60,000(USD) per year and can still learn the same content.
comment_teaching:
On the first day of lecture, I remember Professor Tai saying, "I have taught this course 10 times," so my expectations were quite high. Unfortunately, they fell short. She struggles with pacing her lectures, frequently stumbles over words, and as a result, might skip crucial parts if she runs out of time or takes an extra 10 minutes to finish.

If it weren’t for the in-class quizzes, I would have only attended the tutorials. In fact, I highly recommend attending all tutorials led by Arman. He is clearly passionate about the subject, provides key insights, and emphasizes intuition in his explanations. He might also drop occasional hints about questions that could appear on exams (as he did for the midterms) during the tutorials.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme:

    In-class Quizzes: 18%
    Homework: 12%
    Mid-term Exam: 25%
    Final Exam: 45%

I am writing this review just after taking my final exam, so the final results haven't been released yet (I’ll update this later if I remember). There will be some exchange students also taking this course, so the mean might not accurately reflect the true UST average. Still, it’s quite competitive to score above +1 SD, as indicated by the high standard deviation and self-selection bias (students who choose to take a course during summer vacation may be more motivated than the general population, but that's just my guess).

Midterms:

    Mean: 60.84
    Median: 61
    High: 99
    Upper Quartile: 82.5
    Low: 0
    Lower Quartile: 41

While these scores might seem high compared to a normal semester, the questions were also significantly easier. The true UST mean (if you exclude exchange students) might have been 65+.

Note: I don't hold any prejudice against exchange students; they often spend less time studying and don't stress about grades as much as UST students, since they want to explore Hong Kong.

For this semester, more weight was given to quizzes, but the way the professor conducted them was less than satisfactory. For instance, she would allocate only 3 minutes for some questions that clearly required 5+ minutes of calculations. Additionally, some students skipped lectures and relied on friends to notify them when quizzes would be held. I noticed this because one time there were only 38 students in the class, yet 44 respondents after the quiz ended (simple pigeonhole principle). Given how quickly some students submitted their quizzes and got 100, I suspect they used ChatGPT, which is clearly unfair since quizzes are worth 18% of the grade.
comment_workload: I believe the workload in the summer semester is lighter, as the homework, midterms, and finals are much easier. So, it really depends on the grade you’re aiming for.

author: Thomas Brown
title: Not as bad as people say
comment_content:
I may be biased as I was taught under Mr. Leung's teaching, but the content was not too difficult. The topics taught were:
Logic, Combinations, Probability, Number Theory, Algorithms, Graphs
In terms of difficulty, (in my opinion) they would be arranged (from easiest to hardest)
Logic < Graphs < Algorithms < Number Theory < Combinations < Probability
The topics taught in:
Logic-- are relatively easy, just make sure not to make careless mistakes for these questions
Graphs-- are mostly "proof" questions. They won't ask you much about graph theory as it will be discussed more in year 3
Algorithms-- understand clearly what big O,theta, and omega are, and you're good to go
Number Theory questions are usually about decrypting texts and stuff like that, which are things that you can memorise. So these questions are practically free marks (unless you're careless)
Combinations and Probability-- now these questions are always tricky. A particular TA named Arman always creates creative and tricky questions. You cannot do these questions through sheer memorisation. You have to use your brain and aimlessly think of methods, which is why it is the hardest.

Comp2711 has a telegram group where you can ask questions to the TAs, and usually get an instant response, which I love.
comment_teaching:
I find Kenneth Leung's teachings to be very interesting. He is always very happy when he discusses the topics at hand. He makes these very simple analogies on certain complex topics to make us better understand them. I believe his teaching style is excellent, clear to understand as well as entertaining.

The TAs were all very helpful, especially Arman. Arman, from my experience, replies a lot more in the telegram group than other TAs. He sometimes sends custom questions to the group as extra practice questions for the exam. These custom questions may be hints to certain questions in the exam, so make sure to pay attention to what Arman writes. But Arman is also infamous for making hard questions in exams so :thumbs_up:
comment_grading:
Your grade is affected by a 拉curve
The grade is calculated by: in-class exercises, homework, midterm, final
I don't remember the percentages they take for all of them sorry.
There are around 11 simple in-class exercises, 6 pretty challenging homework, a midterm (25%), a final (40%)

for in-class exercises, my grade was roughly 2/3 each
for homework, my grade was roughly 80-90/100 each
for midterm, my grade was 83/100
for final, my grade was 91/100

In my sem, the midterm was harder than the final ten-fold. So the teachers made the final easier as a promise. So if in your year, the midterm was very hard, just bully the teachers enough to make the finals easier :thumbs_up:
comment_workload:
In terms of the frequency of assignments, it is not a lot. There are only 6 homework to hand in. However, most of the time, the homework would usually take 1 week to finish. I would often find myself doing the homework overnight (like a proper UST student). But I wouldn't say it is "stressful" as there are only 6 homework to hand in.

It's good to keep in mind that the "complexity" of the homework questions is MUCH harder than those in the exams. A lot of us were very scared by the midterm because we thought the midterm questions were as hard as the homework ones, but they weren't really.

comment_teaching:
Prof Kenneth teaches very well. Definitely attend his lectures if possible. Shoutout to TA Arman for holding help desks (impromptu) and explaining the concepts more clearly.

The TAs take turn to teach the tutorials. Most just follows the tutorial notes posted on canvas (i.e. going through the PDF linearly or going through PowerPoints, which still follows the PDF to a T). No offence to the guy, but one of them even spent the whole session writing two whiteboards worth of proof on discrete probability. I thought I was in a MATH course for a second (admittedly this course SHOULD be called MATH2711). I found most tutorials useless, so I skipped most of them.
comment_grading:

Homework: 25% (5 in total, lowest one will be dropped)
Mid-Term: 25%

LQ: 38.25, Median: 51, UQ: 65

LQ: 0, Median: 0, UQ: 1

Finals Statistics

LQ: 52, Median: 70.5, UQ: 84.38

HW & ICE should be full marks but
at last, I've failed myself in the finals.

Finals: 56/100
I'm glad that I got a B- instead of falling to C range. I don't deserve it.
comment_workload:
The content is very difficult. As a year 1 I had to spend a lot of time to digest the concepts. Homeworks are moderate in difficulty. Discussing them with friends/using StackExchange will help a lot.

Exams are fine if you understand the concepts and KNOW how to apply them to solve questions. They're like brain twisters, excluding the computation parts. My brain doesn't handle exam pressure well and I suck at discrete math, so I found them quite difficult.

They made the finals easier by adding a LOT of hints but my brain cracked under the pressure. Bombed the finals.

Past papers are provided on Canvas. If you want more go to Peter Gao or ask seniors.

Well, I guess the good thing is if you take this in year 1, you won't have to deal with this course along with other workload from other courses in year 2+ when you get into CS/CPEG.

author: Datguyfromindo
title: Simple math concepts for CompSci, recommended for Year 1s with strong math background
comment_content:
Before midterm:

Propositional Logic, Predicate Logic, Inference, Sets and Functions, Counting, Combinations, Binomial, Inclusion-Exclusion, Probability, Conditional Probability, Random Variables

After midterm:

Cover the whole content(including the ones before midterm). There is an addition of modulo, gcds, cryptography(mostly about inverse mod, repeating squaring), algorithms, induction and recursion, and graph theory

That might sound a lot, but it's mostly on the basic understandings. If you're confident with your math background(example: great Math A Levels / Math Olympiad) then u should have no problem to take this course ASAP(I took it on my very first sem ;) ) and try to waive for it if needed
comment_teaching:
Papa has a really strong accent. But I like how Papa tries his best to teach, and even at times make his own helpful additional slides containing more practice problems or important concepts that might be ambiguous. Due to this, he usually falls behind Kenneth in terms of pacing. In fact, Kenneth's classes got 1 week of break in the very end, while Papa's class just got into the second half of graph theory, and uses the very final lecture for a review along with a past paper.

However, you can still access Papa's slides and watch his RVC recordings(well, this also apply to Kenneth's) for his lectures, so if you're already confident with Kenneth, you can still know the extra stuff in Papa's classes, and vice versa. I personally say that I prefer Papa due to his depth in teaching, and often times include some stuff from COMP 3711, and ofc he'll point those one out.
comment_grading:
In-Class Exersice 10%
HW 25%
Midterm 25%
Final 40%

You might hate me for my grade, but I can promise u that I'm trying to be as unbiased as possible to write the whole review.

Given Statistics:
Midterm:
Mean: 68.86, stdv: 19.16
Final:
Mean: 66.01, stdv: 19.84

Note that those grades above are before paper checking, and are not updated afterwards

For In-Class Exersice, let's just assume everyone got perfect. HWs tend to have a high average, so don't forget to recheck before submitting yours, especially with the fact that it has the same weighting as midterm

Mine:
In-Class Ex: 10/10
HW: 24.07/25
Midterm: 95/100
Final: 98/100

Got an A+

There's no gradebook given unfortunately, so I can't rly say how good the grading rly is

Tips for exams: the grading might be quite painful if u have some slight mistakes, as most problems are quite straightfoward, and most of the problem require little to none work is needed to be shown, except for some problems like double counting. So, be careful and don't be cocky during the exams.
comment_workload:
There are In-Class Exersices like at the end of every lecture, except a few I guess. I would also say that Papa's In Class is quite fairly easy, and he even gives the major hints and then let us do the rest.

Inappropriate tip: if u think the content is too easy, u can just enter like.... idk 20 minutes before lecture ends, and then do the In-Class Exersice

There are 4 HWs, and each HW would take a fair amount of time, like 2 to 4 hours. Some open sources have also helped me for these questions.

Besides the lectures, in-class exs and HWs, there are also some other helpful resources, such as Kenneth's old lecture videos, tons of supplementary exersices(reviews for midterm & final, and extra problems on several topics), Papa's additional slides as I've mentioned before, tutorial problems, past papers, and several problems from TAs and UGTAs before the final exam.

So... that might sound a ton, but again the concept is quite shallow. So a bit of consistency in the problem solving should be enough

Additionally, I've actually grinded a ton of past papers, but it seems like the past paper is a bit harder than the actual midterm and final. So if u have limited time for this course, u can just trace back on the important concepts from lectures, HWs, tutorials, skim the past papers, and should be fine. Nonetheless, I'd also recommend the past papers for deeper understanding and problem solving of the content

author: Last knight
comment_content: COMP 2711 focuses on essential discrete mathematics concepts tailored for computer science applications, covering enumeration techniques, basic number theory, logic and proofs, recursion and recurrences, probability theory, and graph theory. Students will learn counting principles, permutations, and combinations alongside foundational number theory concepts like divisibility and modular arithmetic, crucial for algorithm design and cryptography. The course emphasizes logical reasoning through various proof techniques, including induction, and explores recursion with methods to solve recurrences relevant to algorithm efficiency. Additionally, it introduces probability theory, addressing random variables and distributions, as well as graph theory, encompassing graph algorithms for searching and optimization. Overall, the course equips students with the mathematical skills necessary for advanced topics in computer science and enhances their problem-solving abilities in a technology-driven landscape.

author: gonpachirou
title: Better introduction to CS than COMP1021
comment_content:
A comprehensive coverage of discrete math topics. My personal favorite is number theory as I've explored it in a bit of depth during high school. A point worth noting, though, is that the second topic requires an ability to write proofs and consequently an understanding of the formal definitions of sets, functions, cartesian products, intersection, union, modus ponens/tollens etc. I spent most of my midterm revision reading about the fundamentals.

Syllabus & textbook chapters
• Logic
○ 1.1-1.3, 1.4-1.5, 1.6, 1.8
• Sets & Functions
○ 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.5
• Counting
○ 6.1, 6.2, 6.3, 6,4, 6.5
○ 8.5, 8.6
• Probability
○ 7.1, 7.2, 7.3
• Expectation and Variance
○ 7.4
• Number Theory
○ 4.1, 4.3-4.5
• Induction and Recursion
• Algorithms & Complexity
comment_teaching:
Kenneth is a great teacher! Super enthusiastic during class and explains concepts clearly. I really enjoyed attending lectures and I think this was the only course in Fall for which I had 100% attendance.

Also happened to be a common core; it was a pleasure to have had the chance to nominate Kenneth for the Teaching Excellence Award.

This might not be the case for all semesters but Kenneth created a telegram group which became insanely active ~2 weeks before the finals due to people discussing questions back and forth. Although I have to say performance in the final exam hugely depends on the amount of personal studying and past paper practice you do, the community this group provided definitely helped clear some things like some algorithm questions.

Kenneth also gamified practice for the final exam. He went out of his way to create a website where students can create their own problems and exchange them with other students' (1 problem you create for 20 problems by other students). I only did 2-3 problems but due to the ample background most students taking this course had, they really aided my revision and also served as fun challenges!

Definitely take this course even if you're not a CS student :)
comment_grading:
• Zoom Polling (10%)
–  There are around 20-24 zoom polls in this course
–  Approximately once every lecture
–  Only count the best 2/3 of them
• Bi-Weekly Written Homework (25%)
–  Late Policy: -1pt for every hour late.
• Midterm Exam (25%)
• Final Exam (40%)

*Note: discussions after the Final Exam with the H students seemed to show that 2711H's exam was somehow easier than ours. Not sure how that was possible but, it is what it is.

Tea (my stats):
- Zoom polls (100%)
- Bi-weekly written homework (100%)
- Midterm exam (71%, lost 20 points in a combinatorics question T_T)
- Final exam (80%)

Final grade: A

Personally I had a good experience with regards to grading, but others seem to say otherwise so I'll leave it for you to decide.
comment_workload:
Manageable, probably less than other math courses like MATH1014 and MATH2111.

Bi-weekly written homeworks consist of ~2 easy questions and ~3 long proof-based questions. You can complete them in 1 hour, 2 hours if you had a bad day.

Revision for finals and midterms >> PAST PAPERS! Do as many as you can and you should get a decent grade!

author: DrainGang
title: Choose the correct instructor!
comment_content:
Math related to CS, some concepts are quite useful (Big-O, mod, RSA).

This course is very much about understanding the course material, hard memorization does nothing. Most of the concepts in this course are not TOO difficult to understand (will take some effort). However, doing the questions in the assignment and exams requires more than understanding the course materials, which is the reason why this course is difficult.

This course is also somehow a common core course, simply put do not even think about taking this course if you are not a CS/CPEG student.
comment_teaching:
Abysmal.

While her English is acceptable, the lectures are very boring and the lecture notes are often incomplete, skipping steps and making the notes very difficult to understand. Concepts are not described clearly and the examples used are far too basic for you to understand what is being asked, some types of questions will not even have examples for you to refer to, meaning you have to Google on your own. And the poor teaching quality is reflected in the exam grades as the mean for my session is significantly lower than the other 2 sessions.

However, since Zoom polls are part of the course, you must attend these lectures and waste 80 minutes of your life sitting there when watching simple Youtube videos on your own makes learning this course far easier.

Tutorials do not require attendance, and all the TAs do is just repeating the answers on the tutorial questions, which you can just do yourself.
comment_grading:
25% Homework: 6 homeworks, only take the score for the best 5. Quite difficult due to the poor teaching quality, luckily some questions have full answers online. Spend time doublechecking your work with your friends to make sure you got the correct answer since its such a big part of the course.

10% Zoom polls: 20 zoom polls, if you answer 10 correctly you get the full 10%. Some luck is required since its unlikely you will understand the lecture enough to answer questions during the same lecture. Mean for this is unknown but it's very likely that most people got all 10%.

25% Midterm: 5 questions, difficulty luckily is lower than the homework questions, but still high. Mean: 29/50

40% Finals: 8 questions, strong emphasis on the materials taught after the midterm, memorizing the way to do the RSA/gcd/mod questions is key because they are very mechanical. Mean: 40.6/80

Ended up with 2% above mean, and got a B, better grading than expected!
comment_workload:
For a 4 credit course it's quite good.

Homework don't take too much time to complete (some questions have full answers online), most of the time is just spent trying to understand the course materials due to the poor teaching quality. However since there are no big projects or assignments, workload is relatively low, with a good lecturer that explains well the workload for this course will be similar to 3 credit MATH course.

As for exams most of the time is spent doing past papers, which the course does provide graciously, doing them is very important as you get to try coming up with solutions to answers yourself, which you don't get to do at all otherwise.

author: K_manifold
title: It can ruin your GPA, or it may not.
comment_content:
Syllabus:
Logic, Set, Permutation, Discrete Probability, Number theory, Recursion, Algorithm analysis

Graph theory is removed from syllabus in this semester.

Logic:
rules of inference, De Morgan's law, proof strategy, mathematical induction etc.
- nothing too complicated
Set:
naïve set theory, basic set operation, inclusion exclusion principle etc.
- basically serves as a foundation for permutation and probability
Permutation:
permutation, combination, ways to put n indistinguishable objects into k boxes etc.
- some you already learnt in secondary school and some require memorisation and understanding
Discrete probability:
expected value, variance, indicator variable, Bayes' theorem etc.
- exam questions can be quite hard
Number theory:
modular arithmetic, Euclidean algorithm, Chinese remainder theorem, discrete logarithm, RSA encryption etc.
- not too complicated, this course mainly covers the practical and simpler aspect of number theory
Recursion:
tower of Hanoi, basic ways to find closed form of certain kind of recursive function etc.
- not hard, you just have to remember the techniques
Algorithm analysis:
big-O notation, complexity analysis, divide-and-conquer, loop, algorithm design (no dynamic programming) etc.
- personally I think it is the most challenging part, but you are expected to be a COMP student.
comment_teaching:
Papadias is a good teacher who tried (and succeeded) to teach well. He provided interesting problems to raise attention on lectures and illustrated the ideas well.

TA will do questions in homework on tutorial sessions, you can ask questions or choose not to attend.
comment_grading:
There are 5 homework, 2 quizzes, 1 final exam and >20 polls (for online lecture).

Grading Scheme:
Homework 30%
Quizzes 15%
Polls 10%
Final Exam 45%

Polls are quite simple, and only the best 2/3 (iirc) polls are counted in final grading.

Each quiz (conducted online, during covid-19) consists of 10 MC questions. MC questions can be very hard.

Average of quiz: 7.6/10

Final exam consists of 2 parts, each part has 3 questions. You are given 60 mins to finish each part. Some questions can be extremely hard. There are always a few people who manage to get full marks though.

Average for final: 32.9/60

Grading is not bad, but the competition is very fierce. Locals should expect to compete with mainland and foreign students who are very competent and hardworking.

Try not to miss any homework and revise well for exams.
comment_workload:
Each homework consists of 5 long questions, and can be done within 2 hours if you are reasonably competent.

Polls are done on lectures and you should complete them without additional efforts.

Workload mainly comes from revision if you intend to get a good grade. The competition is really fierce.

author: Tommy Li
title: Carefulness competition
comment_content:
The course is about maths, discrete maths to be precise. There are mainly 4 topics covered in the semester:

- Counting
- Proofs, Induction and Recursion
- Probability
- Cryptography and Number Theory

The content is quite interesting, like how encryption works (e.g. https SSL sometimes uses RSA to establish a connection) and probability (e.g. the expected no. of tickets we have to buy to win a mark 6). The techniques are useful for future algorithm course like COMP 3711. I believe the inequality version of the master theorem can be proved using the advanced induction technique taught in class, but I did not have the time to do so during my revision period. I did try to expand the general form T(n) = a*T(n/b)+g(n) recurrence (w/ the techniques taught in class) and compared the result with the master theorem.

The topics are not difficult and the course materials suffice for a suitable amount of understanding. The notes explain the concepts well in some details. However, I personally think the notes of the other sessions are more concise and good for revision.
comment_teaching:
Kenneth tried to be funny. He always carries a smile on his face. :)

The teaching is clear. You can understand most of the materials after the lecture. The in-class exercise gives a good practice chance to know your own level of understanding of the materials. A little downside is, the learning atmosphere of the class is not that good and Kenneth only sticks with the course materials. It would better if he can arise our interest to the topic by giving more real-life examples or further readings.
(Sorry for giving a B. I wish to give an A but my grading scheme is not as lenient as Kenneth's :P)  

The TAs explain the pool question quite well, especially Abishek. They are willing to answer your questions too. It's great to have them in the course.
comment_grading:
Kenneth is well known of his lenient grading scheme, this course is not an exception. However, the grade DOES NOT SCALE with your ability.

TBH, I extremely hate the assessments of this course. They are as easy as ABC. You can get full scores in quizzes, midterm and exam as long as you pay attention to the lecture, and most importantly, BEING CAREFUL. The questions in the assessments are extremely similar to the in-class exercises or the pool questions given. You don't even have to think about the solution.

If you aim for an easy B range, or you are a careful person, this course is definitely a good one. Otherwise, consider taking other sections or even H-course, those would better reflect your ability in terms of grades.

All score statistics are publicly available. Sorry for not attaching them here as USTSPACE does not support uploading HTML.

My score:
Quiz 1: 3/3 (Mean: 2.553, SD: 0.597)
Quiz 2: 3/3 (Mean: 2.681, SD: 0.410)
Quiz subtotal: 10/10
Game 1: 5/5 (Mean: 4.725, SD: 0.760)
Game 2: 5/5 (Mean: 4.641, SD: 1.153)
Game subtotal: 10/10
Participation: 10/10
Midterm: 88/100 (Mean: 78.05, SD: 17.81) => 26.4/30
Final: 88/100 (Mean: 81.12, SD: 17.02) => 35.2/40
Overall: 91.6/100 => B+

I misread "or" as "and" losing 8 points in the midterm. Then, I misread "distinguishable" as "indistinguishable" losing 12 points in the final. I understand it is my own fault for not being careful, but what is the point if everybody knows how to do the problems and grades are distributed purely according to carefulness?

I thought I would get a B as I am only in about the 40 percentile in both midterm and final. Thanks to Kenneth's lenient grading scheme, it turns out to be a B+.

Note: The exam papers are different for different sections in 2019 Fall. This may be changed in the future.
comment_workload:
2012H is an 8-credits workload course with 5 credits
2711 is a 2-credits workload course with 4 credits (for Kenneth's class)

I am glad that I have chosen this course else I would be dead from the disastrous workload from 2012H.

Kenneth's section is different from the others. There are only 2 quizzes instead of biweekly/weekly quizzes. There is an extra component which is the gamification platform. You have to answer 5 questions and post 1 question for each round. But there are only 2 rounds.

The quizzes, midterm and exam are extremely easy which you don't have to put extra time for revision (although I did put quite a lot of time, the effort did not pay off). Attend every lesson, complete all in-class exercise (treat it as revision, not for participation score, 12 lectures give you full participation), then you are good to go.

author: GK
title: MATH2711 Funcdament math for computer science
comment_content:
Several major topics:

1. Logic
Basically logics that you would have known even before you study this course. Remember to memorize the notations, which is the only difficult part in this topic.

2. Counting
nPr nCr , you probably had learnt out in secondary school.  

3. Probability
Quite advanced probability.  Some skills will be taught to make calculation easier.  However,it's still like you know it then you know it, you don't know it then you don't know it.

4. Remainder
Basic skill for encryption.  You will learn some method to compute it faster,  and you will learn how to decrypt message under standard RSA cryptosystem.

5. Induction
Based on mathematical induction, you will learn more advanced stuff for Big O calculation.
comment_teaching:

author: Nataliéêè
title: COMP 2711
comment_content: There are five main topic in total. The first three are basically a review of past knowledge. The RSA encryption system is very interesting but the professor didn't go deep into that topic.
comment_teaching: The professor was able to explain the problems very clearly  by using different examples and drawing vivid graphs.
comment_grading: Fair. ( about half SD above mean for each exam --> A-)

author: vgvr
title: Don't take it unless you must
comment_content:
There are a total of five chapters- 1) Logic 2)Combinatorics 3) Probability 4) Number theory and 5) Recursion.

A lot of new concepts are introduced that are pretty hard to grasp. A lot of practice is required to fully understand the concepts. It is beneficial to revise the homework questions and solve questions in past papers before exams. It is important to be in track with the lectures as it is very hard to cover up once you've lagged behind.

The order of contents in the text-book is different and the types of questions given in the textbook is different from ones in exams and assignments. I didn't find it too useful.
comment_teaching: The professor explains the concepts very well and uses examples to illustrate. There is, however, little to no interaction in the class. Make sure you are in track with the content taught in class as it is impossible to do assignments if you're lagging behind.
comment_grading:
Assignments (x5): 10%
Mid-term: 30%
Final: 60%

The questions in both assignments and exams are not MCs and require you to work out the question step by step. The questions are quite hard and slightly vague. It is very hard to know how to start solving the questions without a lot of practice.
It is important to score well in finals due to the large weightage.
comment_workload: The assignments amount to a lot of workload despite being just 10% of the grade. The questions in the tutorial are sometimes similar to the ones in the assignments and a few of the answers can be found by Googling. The rest, however, take a lot of effort and time. You can try e-mailing the TA if you're stuck in any question.

author: RainLi0130
title: Good course, nice TA and teacher
comment_content:
The following key topics are taught:
- Propositional Logic: Including operators, equivalences, proofs using laws (without truth tables), and rules of inference.
- Predicate Logic: Including quantifiers (∀, ∃), expressing statements with predicates, negation of quantified statements, and formal proofs.
- Sets and Functions: Including set operations (intersection, union, complement, subsets), proofs (direct and contrapositive), countability/uncountability of sets (e.g., natural numbers, functions from N to N, binary strings), and bijections.
- Counting Principles: Including permutations, combinations, binomial coefficients, arrangements with restrictions (e.g., adjacency, no two alike adjacent), unordered pairs, palindromes, and paths in graphs (without reusing edges).
- Combinatorial Proofs: Proving identities using combinatorial reasoning (e.g., involving subsets and distinct elements).
- Inclusion-Exclusion Principle: Applied to counting problems (e.g., digits in numbers with specific constraints).
- Pigeonhole Principle: Applied to grid-based problems (e.g., colored cells sharing corners).
- Probability: Including basic probabilities, binomial distributions, and scenarios with independent events (e.g., catching flies).
- Random Variables: Including expectation (E), variance (var), linearity of expectation, independence, and comparisons (e.g., E(X^2) vs. E(XY) for dice rolls).
- Graphs
- MOD
- Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)
comment_teaching: Interesting tutorial: TA discuss hard questions with us in a funny way, leading us to think deeply about a concept.
comment_grading:
Overall kind scoring:
Listening to other classmates, many students took B+ or above and the range of A and B+ are very close.
The difficulty of exams will be easier than the homework, so if you want a good grade, did homework and pp seriously.
comment_workload: all lessons have an in-class exercise but not that difficult, so that's fine. And an assignment with 5-7 exam kind questions every week.

author: sinkie
comment_content: The course was quite manageable overall. The topics were explained clearly, and the pace was reasonable for most students. While there were some challenging parts, the instructors provided helpful guidance and resources.
comment_teaching: The teaching quality was solid. Lectures were structured well and easy to follow. The instructors were approachable and willing to answer questions both in and outside of class.
comment_grading: The grading was fair and transparent. As long as you kept up with the material and prepared for the exams, achieving a good grade was very possible.
comment_workload: The workload was moderate compared to other courses. Assignments and readings were reasonable, and exams tested understanding rather than rote memorization. Overall, the workload was balanced and not overwhelming.

author: ync
title: Math course? hmmmm….
comment_content:
I guess when people hear about things related to math, their first reaction is often like “holy cow, what the heck is that?” But I think this course is different from typical math subjects like calculus. Some content really reminds me of when I was a kid dealing with some intriguing brain teasers brought up by my dad.

Before midterm:
Logic:
L1 Proposition
L2 Predicate
L3 Inference
Sets and Functions:
L4 Sets and Functions
Counting:
L11 Counting
L12 Combinations
L13 Binomial
L14 Inclusion-exclusion

After midterm:
Probability:
L15 Probability
L16 Conditional probability
L17 Random Variable
Number Theory:
L5 Modular arithmetic
L6 Greatest common divisor
L7 Cryptography
Algorithms
L8 Algorithms
Induction and Recursion
L9 Induction
L10 Recursion
Graph
L18 Graph

Personally I prefer the content before midterm as the scenarios of questions related to that are more interesting. Also, for topics like algorithms and graphs, we only explored very fundamental knowledge.
comment_teaching:
The teaching of this course is fabulous for me.
Lecture: Kenneth is good at teaching stuff like that. During the lecture, he can provide interesting examples to help us understand some concepts or theorems.
“I’m bigger than Desmond.” — Kenneth
He is very passionate about teaching and students feel engaged during his lectures. However, his pace is faster compared to other sections, and he sometimes directly skips some slides he deems not important or not difficult. So a brief preview before class is recommended.
Tutorial:
Tutorial is also engaging. This semester, Arman attended most of the tutorials. When he came, we played some interesting yet tricky games. Those who can solve the game can have small prizes. Besides that, the questions in tutorial notes are also well-crafted and suitable as an exercise.
Adding:
They have a telegram group chat where we can discuss exercise questions and share ideas with others, useful for assignments and revision.
comment_grading:
COMP course gives COMP grade— wide B range, but fierce competition for A range, so I may just leave a B here.
10% In-class exercise
25% HW
25% Midterm
40% Final
In-class exercise: It’s more like attendance since the exercise itself is very easy to deal with, and Kenneth even gives some hints so that everyone in the class can manage it. Only 80% of the exercises are counted for grade, which means you can still get the total 10% course grade if you miss 20% of the classes.
HW: totally 4 hw sets and each of them contains around 8 problems. You can spend 2-3 days(6-8 hrs) working through all the questions you can, then discuss the questions that you can’t solve in the group chat, TAs are very friendly and supportive to give hints. Assignments are made easier compared to previous semesters, with the mean of each set around 95.
Midterm: 2 hrs in total, containing around 8 questions. Every question is LQ, you need to provide all the steps of your solution. They will give you scores as long as your logic makes sense. The most challenging questions are about counting, combinatorial proofs. They made the questions easier compared to previous semesters, so the mean is kinda high—68, and SD is 20.
Final: 3 hrs in total, comprises around 10 questions. The format is similar to midterm. The most challenging questions are about probability, modular arithmetic, and algorithms. Final is also easier compared to previous semesters—mean is 70 and SD is 19.
me: total is around 98%, I will change “grading” into E if I don’t get A+. I’m not here to flex and I genuinely hope you guys can all do better than me, so good luck.
comment_workload: Quite light workload considering it’s a comp course. Totally 4 homework sets, each of them can be done in 2-3 days. If you like skipping lectures, you’d better ask your friends to send the in-class exercise to you. If you aim for good grades, you need to spend more time in the exercise so that you can familiarize yourself with what formula/algorithm/rule to apply during exams.

author: khumo
title: The study of computer science necessitates a foundational understanding of discrete mathematics, encompassing fundamental topics such as enumeration techniques, basic number theory, logic and proofs, recursion and recurrences, probability theory, and graph theory. comment_content: This course is challenging. Adequate preparation does not guarantee a high grade. But it is manageable. You must use your logic!
comment_teaching: Arya, Sunil is one of the best professors I have ever met. His teaching skills are so good. But there were no video recordings of this class. Instead, they gave us new professor video recordings. These recordings show me Sunil is so good one more time.
comment_grading: Grading is fine. If you get mean, you can get B-. Midterm is easy. But final is going to be so hard.
comment_workload: The workload is manageable. The fundamental objective of this course is to assess one's comprehension. The efficacy of diligent effort is rendered futile in the absence of linguistic comprehension. This training is specifically tailored for bright individuals rather than diligent workers.

author: Frank2416
comment_content:
The content before midterm is not difficult. Started with operators; however, content after midterm becomes a bit challenging.
Logic
comment_teaching: Attending lectures is very beneficial in understanding topics. Professor helps us understand concepts. Also there are in class exercises that count towards final grade.
comment_grading:
In-class Quizzes: 10%
Homework 20%
Mid-term Exam 25%
Final Exam 45%
In-class quizzes will be conducted by Canvas Quizzes.  The 20% lowest scores (including no submissions)  will be dropped in calculating your final quiz score.  Quizzes conducted during the first two weeks (add/drop period) will not be counted.

There will be 6 homework assignments throughout the course.  The lowest score (including no submission) will be dropped in calculating your final homework score.

Homework late Policy: 1 point for every hour late.

For the midterm and final exam, you will be allowed to bring one (midterm) or two (final) A4-sized cheat sheets. It can be either single-sided or double-sided, handwritten or printed.

The exams may have optional bonus questions that are more challenging than the normal questions. The final letter grades will be first assigned without counting these bonus questions.  Then the bonus questions will be added to your final score for possible grade promotion (at most one sub-grade).

comment_teaching: Kenneth is the best professor I have met in ust so far. He explains his slides very well with examples and the lesson is interesting. He is willing to answer students' questions at any time with the telegram group. The TA and some students in the group are so active in answering questions and giving out more example to help us understand better. They are all excellent and the atmosphere is so good.
comment_grading:

Midterm 25%
Final 40%

The grading is very good (maybe due to Kenneth's nice teaching), a score around mean should be enough to get B. For some reason, there are a lot of year-1 students in this session but don't be too happy as they end up taking a lot of good grades. The polling is easy and you should be able to get full if you have common sense. The mean of the homework is quite high so make sure to pay extra attention to them.
